era_context: The early 16th century saw the Portuguese maritime empire expanding under Manuel I, the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II, the Ming Dynasty in China under the Zhengde Emperor, and the Mughal Empire emerging under Babur. The Italian Renaissance was at its height with major artistic and intellectual achievements. In the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires remained at their territorial peaks. This was a period of significant global transformation, with European expansion beginning to reshape trade networks and geopolitical relationships.

events.title: Waldseemüller publishes world map naming 'America'
events.description: Martin Waldseemüller, a German cartographer, published a world map (Universalis Cosmographia) that included the newly discovered lands of the Western Hemisphere. Crucially, Waldseemüller labeled the new continent 'America' in honor of Amerigo Vespucci, the Italian explorer who had explored the South American coast. This map was widely distributed and influential, and the name 'America' gradually became the standard designation for the continents. The publication of this map marked a significant moment in the European understanding of global geography and the recognition of the Americas as a distinct landmass.
events.certainty_note: The publication of Waldseemüller's map in 1507 is well-documented. The naming of 'America' on this map is confirmed by surviving copies.

events.title: Portuguese expansion in Indian Ocean under Afonso de Albuquerque
events.description: Afonso de Albuquerque continued his campaigns to establish Portuguese dominance in the Indian Ocean. In 1507, he was consolidating Portuguese control over key ports and trade routes. Albuquerque's strategy involved capturing strategic coastal cities and establishing fortified trading posts. His campaigns were characterized by aggressive naval tactics and the use of superior European naval technology. By 1507, Albuquerque had become the dominant Portuguese military figure in Asia.
events.certainty_note: Albuquerque's campaigns in 1507 are documented in Portuguese sources, though specific events are less precisely dated.

events.title: Pope Julius II's reign and military campaigns
events.description: Pope Julius II continued his reign as head of the Catholic Church and temporal ruler of the Papal States. Julius II was known for his aggressive military campaigns to expand and consolidate papal territory in Italy. He was also a major patron of the arts, commissioning major works including the rebuilding of St. Peter's Basilica and Michelangelo's work on the Sistine Chapel. In 1507, Julius II was consolidating his power and planning his military and cultural initiatives.
events.certainty_note: Pope Julius II's reign dates and activities are well-documented in papal records.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's reign in Ming China
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor continued his reign, characterized by increasing eunuch influence and the emperor's personal indulgence. The eunuch Liu Jin wielded enormous power over state affairs. Despite governance issues, the Ming Dynasty maintained economic prosperity and cultural vitality. The period saw continued artistic and literary achievements, though political stability was compromised by the emperor's disinterest in traditional governance.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign and eunuch influence are well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Babur's military campaigns in Central Asia
events.description: Babur, the future founder of the Mughal Empire, was engaged in military campaigns to expand his territory in Central Asia. He was consolidating power and eliminating rival sultanates. Babur's military innovations, including the use of gunpowder artillery and cavalry tactics, were beginning to reshape warfare in the region. His campaigns in this period laid the groundwork for his eventual conquest of northern India.
events.certainty_note: Babur's campaigns in this period are documented in his autobiography, though specific events in 1507 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The naming of 'America' by Waldseemüller is sometimes attributed to other cartographers or claimed to be a later development. However, modern scholarship confirms that Waldseemüller's 1507 map was the first to use the name 'America' for the continents. The significance of the map is sometimes overstated; the name did not immediately become universal and took decades to become standard. The map itself was not widely distributed in its time, though surviving copies indicate its influence on later cartographers.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1507 is rich, particularly for European, Ottoman, and Chinese sources. European cartographic and papal records are detailed. Portuguese colonial records are comprehensive. Chinese sources are detailed. However, sources from the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia are less abundant in European archives. The perspective is heavily weighted toward European and Chinese court records. Indigenous perspectives from conquered regions and the Americas are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Waldseemüller's map represents the culmination of European knowledge following Columbus's voyages
Pope Julius II's patronage led to Michelangelo's commission for the Sistine Chapel in 1508
